chunk_summary: This section outlines dysphagia safety screening and red flag findings during dietary and feeding skills evaluation, emphasizing risk reduction strategies and early referral for specialist assessment in high-risk pediatric groups.
learning_objectives: Identify red flag symptoms of dysphagia during feeding evaluations.
Understand risk factors for unsafe swallowing in high-risk pediatric populations.
Learn immediate risk reduction strategies for children with suspected dysphagia.
Recognize the importance of early referral to SLP and multidisciplinary feeding teams.
key_facts: Aspiration may present as poor growth, recurrent wheeze, or pneumonia rather than choking.
Prolonged meal duration (>30 minutes for infants) is a red flag for dysphagia.
High-risk groups include preterm infants, children with neuromotor disorders, craniofacial anomalies, genetic syndromes, CHD, or chronic lung disease.
Avoid thin liquids if they trigger coughing; prioritize upright positioning and slower feeding pace.
Empiric thickening or texture restriction should not be a long-term plan without evaluation.
reference_quote: Feeding skill evaluation must include a safety screen for dysphagia because aspiration can present as poor growth, recurrent wheeze, or pneumonia rather than obvious choking. (Source: ASHA Practice Portal)
document_key_topics: Risk reduction strategies for unsafe swallowing
